summary: No-ship: the RED proof and production call seam are sound, but the absence classifier can mistake a replacement `.objects` directory for the original container. Simpler alternative: apply one shared absence classifier at the existing call sites without the Entry/Hook refactor; either design still requires stable parent identity. Open question: none.

findings.title: Replacement directory can forge the parent-liveness proof
findings.body: `metadata(parent).is_dir()` proves only that some directory currently occupies the pathname, not that the snapshotted parent survived. If `.objects` is removed or renamed after the snapshot and a concurrent writer recreates it, `symlink_metadata(p)` returns NotFound while the new parent passes `container_alive`; the plan then mints `Absent`, skips entries from the destroyed container, and can publish `Ok(stats)` plus a new pending ledger. This violates the stated single flip and P5 promise that container destruction remains a loud error. W10 only tests deletion without recreation, so it cannot catch this ABA race.
findings.recommendation: Capture the parent directory's stable identity when taking each snapshot and mint `Absent` only if the same directory still exists—prefer an open directory handle/file ID, or compare platform file identity and conservatively propagate where unsupported. Add a W10 variant that removes or renames `.objects`, recreates it through a concurrent write, and requires the original NotFound with no pending-file publication.

next_steps: Revise the absence proof and preserved-contract tests to reject `.objects` delete/recreate and rename/recreate ABA scenarios.
